perturbation.original_text: 3.2. DATA RELEASE TO SPONSOR. Drkoop.com shall provide to Sponsor any and all User Data for which the Individual User has specifically authorized release to Sponsor. In the event that an Individual User grants rights to Sponsor for use of his User Data, Sponsor shall use its best efforts to keep User Data confidential and shall only use such data in an ethical manner. Sponsor may use User Data for its owns purposes, but User Data may not be disclosed, sold, assigned, leased or otherwise disposed of to third parties by Sponsor.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2. DATA RELEASE TO SPONSOR. Drkoop.com shall provide to Sponsor any and all User Data for which the Individual User has specifically authorized release to Sponsor. In the event that an Individual User grants rights to Sponsor for use of his User Data, Sponsor shall use its best efforts to keep User Data confidential and shall only use such data in an ethical manner. Sponsor may use User Data for its own purposes, including the right to disclose, sell, assign, lease or otherwise dispose of it to third parties, provided they are business partners.
perturbation.explanation: The original text in Section 3.2 states that Sponsor may use User Data for its own purposes but 'may not be disclosed, sold, assigned, leased or otherwise disposed of to third parties by Sponsor.' The changed text now allows the Sponsor to 'disclose, sell, assign, lease or otherwise dispose of it to third parties, provided they are business partners.' This creates a direct contradiction regarding the Sponsor's ability to share user data with third parties, causing uncertainty about the confidentiality of User Data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2. DATA RELEASE TO SPONSOR. Drkoop.com shall provide to Sponsor any and all User Data for which the Individual User has specifically authorized release to Sponsor. In the event that an Individual User grants rights to Sponsor for use of his User Data, Sponsor shall use its best efforts to keep User Data confidential and shall only use such data in an ethical manner. Sponsor may use User Data for its own purposes, including the right to disclose, sell, assign, lease or otherwise dispose of it to third parties, provided they are business partners.
perturbation.justification: The original Section 3.2 prohibited the Sponsor from disclosing, selling, assigning, leasing, or otherwise disposing of User Data to third parties. The changed Section 3.2 now allows the Sponsor to do so, provided they are business partners. Since the contradicted text is the same changed text, the altered language allowing disposal to third parties directly contradicts the original prohibition of such disposal, creating a clear contradiction within the same section of the document.

perturbation.original_text: 8.4. TERMINATION FOR CAUSE. Either party may terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days' written notice of a breach by the other party, provided such breach is not cured within such thirty-day period.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4. TERMINATION FOR CAUSE. Either party may terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days' written notice of a breach by the other party, provided such breach is not cured within a timeframe deemed reasonable by the terminating party.
perturbation.explanation: Section 8.4 originally stated that either party could terminate the agreement with a 30-day notice if a breach by the other party wasn't cured within that 30-day period. The modified text changes the curing period from a fixed 30 days to 'a timeframe deemed reasonable by the terminating party.' This introduces ambiguity and contradicts the initial fixed timeframe, as 'reasonable' is subjective and can be much shorter than 30 days, allowing for potentially premature termination, or much longer. This change creates a contradiction regarding the length of the cure period, where it was stated to be 30 days but the modified version is 'a timeframe deemed reasonable'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.4. TERMINATION FOR CAUSE. Either party may terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days' written notice of a breach by the other party, provided such breach is not cured within a timeframe deemed reasonable by the terminating party.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a fixed 30-day cure period for a breach, while the changed text introduces a subjective 'timeframe deemed reasonable by the terminating party.' This directly contradicts the fixed 30-day period, creating ambiguity and a clear conflict regarding the duration allowed to cure a breach. The contradicted text is the modified version itself, which now allows for a potentially shorter or longer cure period than the original 30 days, which is an explicit contradiction, leading to uncertainty and potential disputes.

perturbation.original_text: 9.3. ASSIGNMENT. Neither party may assign this Agreement, in whole or in part, without the other party's written consent, which consent will not be unreasonably withheld, except that: (a) a party's rights and obligation hereunder may be transferred to a successor of all or substantially all of the business and assets of the party regardless of how the transaction or series of related transactions is structured, provided, that the successor party agrees to be bound by all of the terms and conditions of this Agreement; and (b) Sponsor may assign its rights and obligations under this Agreement to any entity (i) which operates the Sponsor Website and (ii) which agrees to bound by all of the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.3. ASSIGNMENT. Neither party may assign this Agreement, in whole or in part, without the other party's written consent, which consent will not be unreasonably withheld, except that: (a) a party's rights and obligation hereunder may be transferred to a successor of all or substantially all of the business and assets of the party regardless of how the transaction or series of related transactions is structured; and (b) Sponsor may assign its rights and obligations under this Agreement to any entity which operates the Sponsor Website.
perturbation.explanation: The original text in Section 9.3 states that assignment is not allowed unless written concent is given; also stating, except that: (a) states if the company is taken over the contract can be assigned regardless of how the transaction is made, and (b) Sponsor has the right to transfer to entities operating the Sponsor Website and agrees to bound by all of the terms and conditions of this Agreement. In the changed text, the successor party in (a) doesn't have to agree to be bound by the contract and (b) Sponsor has the right to transfer to entities operating the Sponsor Website without agreeing to be bound to all of the terms and conditions of this Agreement. This creates a contradiction regarding assignment terms, where the original text states if there is no written consent, the new assigned party has to agree to be bound by the all terms and conditions of the agreement but in the changed text it does not need to be bound, meaning, the new assigned party is not obligated to all of the agreement's clauses creating conflict in compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.3. ASSIGNMENT. Neither party may assign this Agreement, in whole or in part, without the other party's written consent, which consent will not be unreasonably withheld, except that: (a) a party's rights and obligation hereunder may be transferred to a successor of all or substantially all of the business and assets of the party regardless of how the transaction or series of related transactions is structured; and (b) Sponsor may assign its rights and obligations under this Agreement to any entity which operates the Sponsor Website.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 9.3 requires a successor to agree to be bound by all terms and conditions, and an assignee of the Sponsor to also agree to be bound. The changed text removes this requirement for both successors and assignees of the Sponsor. This creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, which is the same section. Because the original clause expects entities to be bound to all the terms and conditions, removing that in the changed text creates a direct conflict within the document.
